title: Reuben B. Knowles (1817-1885): A Lifelong Farmer of Troy, Maine
narrative: Reuben B. Knowles was born in 1817 in Troy, in what would become Waldo County, Maine. When he was born the town was only a few years old and had not yet settled on its name: chartered in 1812 as Kingville out of the area earlier known as Bridge's (or Bridgetown) Plantation and Township No. 4, First Range, it was renamed Joy in 1815, Montgomery in 1826, and finally Troy in 1827, the same year the surrounding country was organized as Waldo County out of parts of Hancock, Kennebec, and Lincoln Counties. Reuben thus grew up alongside the town itself, in a raw, recently incorporated farming community in the Maine interior.

His parents were John Franklin Knowles (1784-1873) and Catherine Sprague. John Franklin Knowles is associated with Readfield, in Kennebec County, Maine, which lies inland to the west of Troy; like many families of that generation, the Knowleses appear to have moved eastward and northward into the newly opening towns of the Waldo County backcountry as land there was surveyed, granted, and brought under cultivation in the first decades of the nineteenth century. The migration of a young couple from an established Kennebec town into a frontier plantation that was still changing its name fits the broad, well-documented pattern of interior Maine settlement in this era, though the specific circumstances and exact year of the family's arrival in Troy are not established in the free records consulted here.

Reuben married Elizabeth S. Sprague. The repetition of the Sprague surname, shared by his mother Catherine Sprague, reflects how tightly the founding families of a small Maine farm town were bound together; in communities of a few hundred people, neighbors intermarried across a handful of surnames over successive generations. Beyond the fact of the marriage and Elizabeth's name, the documentary detail available from free sources is thin, and specific dates for the marriage and for the births of any children are not confirmed here and are therefore left open rather than guessed at.

By every indication Reuben lived the life of a Troy farmer. Troy in the nineteenth century was a town of scattered family farms and named rural neighborhoods - East Troy, North Troy, West Troy, Troy Center, and a scatter of 'corners' such as Cooks, Gerrish, Greens, Hales, Mitchell, Rogers, and Smarts - with no industry of consequence beyond what the soil, the woodlot, and the local mills could yield. A man of Reuben's generation there would have practiced the mixed subsistence farming typical of interior Maine: hay, oats, potatoes, a few cattle and sheep, a kitchen garden, and home production of goods that could not be bought. This is the general context of his working life rather than a record of his particular acreage or crops, which the free sources do not detail.

Reuben's adult years spanned the Civil War, which fell heavily on rural Maine. Maine sent a disproportionately large share of its men into the Union armies, and small Waldo County farm towns like Troy contributed soldiers, money, and bereavement out of all proportion to their size. Reuben himself would have been in his forties during the war years of 1861-1865 - older than most who enlisted - and the free records consulted do not show military service for him; the more likely shape of his war was the home-front one shared by older farmers everywhere in Maine, keeping a farm and a town running while younger neighbors and kinsmen went south. The war is offered here as the defining national event of his middle age, not as a documented chapter of his personal story.

Reuben B. Knowles died in 1885, in the same town of Troy where he had been born sixty-eight years earlier, a genuinely lifelong resident in a region where many of his contemporaries left for the cities, the mills, or the open lands of the West. He is buried in Mount Holly Cemetery in Troy, Waldo County, Maine, where his dates of 1817 and 1885 are recorded. His father, John Franklin Knowles, had died only a dozen years before him, in 1873, having lived to nearly ninety - a reminder that the Knowles family put down deep and durable roots in this corner of Maine.

What can be said with confidence about Reuben B. Knowles is modest but solid: a man born in 1817 in the young town of Troy, son of John Franklin Knowles and Catherine Sprague, husband of Elizabeth S. Sprague, a farmer who lived his whole life in Troy and was buried there at his death in 1885. The larger texture of his days - the cycles of a subsistence farm, the intermarried web of Troy's founding families, and the shadow of the Civil War over rural Maine - is drawn from the documented history of his town and county rather than from records naming him directly, and is presented here as context, not as invented biography.
